content:
7 min read
1 hour ago

--

We used javascript a lot in both frontend and backend and when we prepare for an interview or we are in technical discussion we heard a lot saying that “Javascript is single threaded”.
But then you also write code like this every day:
setTimeout(() => console.log("Hello after 2 seconds"), 2000);
fetch("https://api.example.com/data").then(data => console.log(data));This looks like JavaScript is doing many things at once. So is it single-threaded or not?
To make this super clear, let’s imagine JavaScript as one restaurant with one chef. This one story will explain everything: single-threading, async code, and Workers.
1. What Does “Single-Threaded” Mean?
Imagine a small restaurant kitchen. There is only one chef. The chef can only cook one dish at a time. He cannot chop vegetables for dish A and fry dish B at the exact same second — he does one thing, finishes it , then moves to the next.
This is how exactly javascript is work:
JavaScript has only one call stack — one place where code runs. At any single moment, only one line of your code is executing. Nothing else runs at the same time as it.
That one chef = the JavaScript engine. There’s no second chef standing next to him cooking a different dish in parallel.
2. Why did javascript choose to have only one chef?
JavaScript was built to run inside web pages — clicking buttons, showing menus, updating text on screen. This is called the DOM (the actual webpage you see).
Imagine if this restaurant had 5 chefs, and all 5 chefs were allowed to touch the same plate at the same time one adding sauce, another removing rice, another adding salt all at once. The plate would become a mess. Nobody would know the final result.
That’s what would happen to your webpage if multiple threads could change it at the same time. So the creators of JavaScript made a simple decision:

• One chef, one plate at a time → No confusion, no messy webpage, no complicated locks or rules for multiple chefs to avoid bumping into each other.

• It made the language simple and safe for beginners and browsers.
This was a design choice — not a bug, not a limitation someone forgot to fix.
3. Then How does javascript handle many things “at once”?
So here is the twist: the chef doesn’t work alone in an empty kitchen. The restaurant also has waiters.
Okay so let’s understand it with below example code:
console.log("Order taken: Salad"); // Chef starts immediatelysetTimeout(() => {
console.log("Order taken: Pizza is ready!");
}, 2000); // Chef hands this to a waiter and moves on
fetch("https://api.example.com/menu") // Chef hands this to another waiter too
.then(res => console.log("Order taken: Menu fetched!"));
console.log("Order taken: Water served"); // Chef continues immediately
Output order for above example : salad -> water served -> (later) menu fetched -> pizza is ready.
Notice: the chef never waited around doing nothing for 2 seconds. Here’s what really happened:

• The chef (JS engine) got the “make pizza in 2 seconds” order. Instead of standing at the oven for 2 minutes staring at the timer, he gave it to a waiter (this is the browser or Node.js, not JavaScript itself) and immediately moved to the next customer.

• The waiter sets a timer, or goes to the network to fetch the menu, and waits outside the kitchen — not blocking the chef at all.

• Once the timer finishes or the data arrives, the waiter doesn’t run into the kitchen and interrupt the chef mid-dish. Instead, he places the order in a queue by the kitchen door.

• There’s a manager standing at the door who keeps checking: “Is the chef free right now?” The moment the chef is free, the manager hands him the next order from the queue.
This manager is called the Event Loop. The waiters and their tools (timers, network calls, file reading) are called Web APIs (in browsers) or libuv (in Node.js).
The chef (JavaScript) is still doing only ONE thing at a time. But the restaurant around him the waiters, the queue, the manager is handling waiting-type work in the background, so the chef never gets stuck idle. This makes JavaScript feel like it’s doing many things together, even though only one line of your actual code ever runs at any instant.
This is why a giant for loop that takes 10 seconds to finish will freeze your entire webpage — the chef is stuck doing that one long task, and the manager can't hand him anything else, not even "someone clicked a button," until he's done.
4. So why do we still call it “single threaded” if waiter exists?
Because the waiters never cook. They only carry orders, wait for timers, or fetch things from the network. They cannot touch a pan or make a dish. Only the one chef ever actually cooks — meaning only the JS engine ever actually runs your JavaScript code.
So:

• Async (non-blocking waiting) ✅ — yes, JavaScript is great at this.

• Parallel (two pieces of your code running at the exact same instant) ❌ — no, this never happens with just the chef and waiters.
To get real parallel cooking — two dishes literally being cooked at the same second — you need something bigger: a second kitchen with a second chef. That’s what Workers give you.
5. Web workers & worker threads : Hiring a second chef
Now imagine the restaurant opens a second, completely separate kitchen next door, with its own chef, own stove, own ingredients. This second kitchen can cook a dish at the exact same time as the first kitchen. That’s real parallelism.
This is exactly what a Web Worker (in browsers) or Worker Thread (in Node.js) is:
// main.js — the main kitchen
const worker = new Worker("worker.js");
worker.postMessage({ number: 10 });worker.onmessage = (e) => {
console.log("Second kitchen finished:", e.data);
};
// worker.js — the second, separate kitchen
onmessage = (e) => {
const result = e.data.number * e.data.number;
postMessage(result); // send the finished dish back
};
Some of important rules about this second kitchen:
It has no access to the first kitchen’s plates, ingredients, or tools directly (no shared DOM, no shared variables). It’s a fully separate room.
To send a dish (data) between kitchens, a waiter carries a copy of it over (postMessage). The original kitchen keeps its own copy too. This is safe — no two chefs are ever fighting over the same plate.
This is perfect for heavy cooking — like slow-roasting something for 10 minutes (heavy computation: image processing, big calculations, parsing huge files) — without making the main chef’s customers wait.
Node.js has the same idea via the worker_threads module, used when a heavy calculation would otherwise freeze your server for every user.
6. Shared array buffer: two kitchens, one shared plate
Normally, the two kitchens only pass copies of dishes to each other. But sometimes, copying a huge dish back and forth is slow and wasteful. So JavaScript offers something risky but powerful: one single shared plate that both kitchens can literally touch at the same time.
That’s a SharedArrayBuffer.
const sab = new SharedArrayBuffer(4); // the shared plate
const view = new Int32Array(sab);worker.postMessage(sab); // both kitchens now see the SAME plate, not a copy
This is powerful, but dangerous — exactly the mess we avoided in Section 2. If both chefs reach for the same shared plate at the same time — one adding salt while the other removes rice — you get inconsistent, broken results. This is called a race condition.
Because of this danger (and some security concerns around timing attacks), browsers actually disabled SharedArrayBuffer for a while and only allow it today on pages set up with special security headers.
7. Atomics: Kitchen Traffic rules for the shared plate
If two chefs must share one plate, they need traffic rules — like a “only one chef touches the plate at a time” sign. That’s what Atomics provides.
Atomics.add(view, 0, 1); // safely add 1 to the shared plate — no interruptions mid-action
Atomics.store(view, 0, 10); // safely set a value
Atomics.load(view, 0); // safely read a valueAtomics also lets one chef wait and pause until the other chef signals "I'm done, your turn":
Atomics.wait(view, 0, 0); // Chef 2 waits here until told to continue
Atomics.notify(view, 0, 1); // Chef 1 signals "go ahead now!"Without these traffic rules, sharing one plate between two real chefs (real threads) is just asking for chaos. Atomics is what makes the shared plate usable instead of dangerous.
Conclusion:

• JavaScript is single-threaded because it only ever has one chef cooking your actual code, one line at a time.

• It feels like it does many things at once because of waiters and a manager (Web APIs + Event Loop) — they handle waiting in the background, not your actual JS code.

• This is concurrency, not parallelism — many things “in progress,” but never two lines of your JS running at the literal same instant.

• Workers are the real exception — they open a second kitchen with its own chef, giving you true parallel execution, kept safely separate by default.

• SharedArrayBuffer lets two kitchens share one plate directly, and Atomics provides the traffic rules to make that sharing safe.
So next time someone says “JavaScript is single-threaded,” you can say: “Yes — one chef. But the restaurant around him has waiters, a manager, and even a second kitchen next door if you really need one.”
